title: The Politics of Foreclosures
abstract: The U.S. House of Representatives Financial Services Committee considered many important banking reforms in 2009 to 2010. We show that, during this period, foreclosure starts on delinquent mortgages were delayed in the districts of committee members although there was no difference in delinquency rates between committee and noncommittee districts. In these areas, banks delayed the foreclosure starts by 0.5 months (relative to the 12-month average). The estimated cost of delay to lenders is an order of magnitude greater than the campaign contributions by the political action committees of the largest mortgage servicing banks to the committee members in that period.

article.introduction:
The financial crisis of2008 led to a sharp rise in foreclosures. As a highly visible symbol of the crisis, foreclosures attracted considerable attention from politicians, regulators, and the public. In particular, large banks—many of which also service securitized mortgages—and their foreclosure practices faced public scrutiny. For example, during a congressional hearing on February 10, 2009, Barney Frank, then-Chairman of the Financial Services Committee in the U.S. House of Representatives (HFSC), called for a moratorium on new foreclosure starts.1Within days, at least one very large bank agreed to delay any new foreclosures.2Foreclosures remained a focal point of political discussion in the following months.
Our paper focuses on whether financial institutions’ decisions with regard to starting the foreclosure process were systematically related to not only economic concerns, but also political concerns. Congress took many major legislative actions over the 2009 to 2010 period in relation to the financial sector, culminating in the passage of the Dodd-Frank Act. The financial industry was thus highly motivated to follow legislative developments and to adjust some of its practices in anticipation of changes.
The literature on campaign contributions and lobbying proposes a number of possible reasons why banks might attempt to influence the political process through their foreclosure actions.3First, foreclosure delays may help decrease pressure on politicians from their voters and allow banks to obtain more favorable legislative outcomes.4Second, a lenient approach to foreclosure in the districts of powerful politicians may help banks gain access to these politicians.5Third, such delays may help politicians sympathetic to the banks’ perspective get reelected.6Finally, politicians themselves may pressure banks for leniency on delinquent borrowers in their district, as Barney Frank's call for a moratorium on foreclosures suggests. Naturally, these reasons are not mutually exclusive and possibly not exhaustive.
The foreclosure process on delinquent loans starts only when a lender—or its agent, the loan servicer—takes explicit action. Thestartof the foreclosure process is therefore largely discretionary and can be delayed. To identify political motivations in banks’ foreclosure decisions, we follow the political economy literature, which emphasizes the importance of the HFSC for laws related to the financial sector,7and study whether banks delayed foreclosure initiations on delinquent mortgages in the districts of HFSC members during 2009 to 2010, when important legislation related to the financial sector was being considered. We use institutional details of the U.S. Congress in our test design. For example, given the importance of seniority in congressional committees, incumbents tend to stay on the same committee for multiple terms. Hence, most members of the HFSC decided to join this committee long before the onset of the financial crisis.
We find that mortgage-servicing banks did indeed delay the start of foreclosures on delinquent loans if those loans were located in the electoral districts of HFSC members. Importantly, there was no difference in delinquency rates across committee districts, so this differential delay cannot be attributed to servicers’ capacity constraints under a large volume of delinquent loans. These results are robust to the inclusion of many loan- and location-specific controls, some of which are time-varying (e.g., zip code–level house price changes), as well as to the inclusion of any state-specific time effects.
The average time to foreclosure starts in noncommittee districts is about 12 months, taking into account the right-censoring at the sample end. In contrast, in committee districts, this average is about half a month longer. Based on foreclosure cost estimates from the literature and using only the aggregate value of delinquent loans in our sample, we conservatively estimate the direct cost of delay to lenders to be about $30 million. Although this cost may be small in the context of the mortgage market, it should be evaluated relative to other political actions by large banks, as the importance of banks’ political activities is not captured by the direct impact of their cost on bank earnings but rather by their potential to influence the political process. During the sample period, the top 10 mortgage servicers, which include some of the largest financial institutions (e.g., Bank of America, Citigroup, JPMorgan, and Wells Fargo) collectively spent approximately $44 million for lobbying members of the legislative and executive branches, not just HFSC members, while the combined campaign contributions to HFSC members by these banks’ political action committees amounted to roughly $1 million. Thus, for the 10 largest servicers, the cost of foreclosure delays in committee districts is comparable to their lobbying costs and an order of magnitude greater than their campaign contributions to committee members.
Delaying foreclosure starts is a novel channel for banks to influence the political process. Despite the similarities of this channel's cost to the lobbying expenses of these servicers, there are also important differences between this channel and the more traditional political activities through lobbying and contributing to campaigns. First, by law, lobbying expenditures cannot be channeled to politicians’ campaigns. The foreclosure delays, in contrast, directly benefit the constituents of committee members. Campaign contributions, of course, can be used in politicians’ campaigns, but foreclosure delays may be able to target politicians’ voters more precisely than some of the campaigning activities that are paid by contributions. For example, while television advertisements are used heavily in political campaigns, their coverage is likely to extend beyond the relevant district's borders, especially in urban areas; by contrast, foreclosure delays can accurately target voters in a particular district. Second, unlike campaign contributions, foreclosure delays are not subject to campaign contribution limits and do not have to be disclosed.
We verify that our results are not spurious through a variety of tests. As placebo tests, we check the membership in the House Transportation & Infrastructure and Armed Services committees and find no link between membership in either of those two committees and the timing of foreclosures. We also do not find any effect of HFSC membership in earlier years, when foreclosures did not attract as much attention. In addition, if the effect we detect were due to spurious correlation, one might expect HFSC membership in 2009 to 2010 to influence foreclosure decisions before 2009, but we find no such effect.
To address the concern that legislators may self-select into the HFSC based on the delinquency rates of their constituents, we restrict our sample to legislators who were elected to the House and to the HFSC before 2005, well before the onset of the crisis. We find an even stronger effect of committee membership for this subsample. We also find similar results when the noncommittee districts are restricted to those near the committee districts. Interestingly, when we check for differences in delinquency rates, we do not find any between committee and noncommittee districts. Given the consistency of our robustness results, we interpret the delay in mortgage foreclosures as due to loan servicing banks’ attempt to influence the political process.
Our paper is related to three strands of the literature. First, a growing body of work explores various aspects of mortgage market practices. In contrast to a large set of literature that evaluates precrisis market developments—see, for instance, Keys et al. (2010,2012), Mian and Sufi (2009), Adelino, Schoar, and Severino (2016), Mayer, Pence, and Sherlund (2009), Jiang, Nelson, and Vytlacil (2014a,2014b), and Agarwal, Chang, and Yavas (2012)—we focus on the aftermath of the crisis. As such, our paper is also closely related to studies that explore lenders’ or their agents’ approaches to the mitigation of delinquent mortgage losses, which surged during the crisis period. These studies include work by Piskorski, Seru, and Vig (2010), Agarwal et al. (2011), and Zhang (2013), who compare the start of foreclosures for portfolio loans with that for securitized loans and focus on potential agency problems. Agarwal et al. (2017) evaluate the effects of the Home Affordable Modification Program, which offered mortgage modifications to millions of borrowers. Our focus is on the political motivations behind banks’ approach to delinquent mortgages.
Second, our paper contributes to the literature on the political influence of corporations, which focuses largely on campaign contributions and lobbying.8By focusing on a nontraditional political activity, our paper is similar to Bertrand et al. (2007), who find that politically connected firms distort their labor practices to help favored politicians in elections in France.9
Finally, our paper is related to the literature on the political economy of finance, in particular, to studies that examine the role of politics in the financial crisis and its aftermath.10Mian, Sufi, and Trebbi (2010) demonstrate that representatives of districts with high default rates were more likely to vote for the Foreclosure Prevention Act in the U.S. House of Representatives. Igan, Mishra, and Tressel (2009) show that lenders that lobbied more for the relaxation of rules pertaining to securitization and consumer protection subsequently increased their mortgage lending more and originated loans with higher loan-to-income ratios. Duchin and Sosyura (2010) argue that banks with headquarters located in the district of a member of the HFSC were more likely to receive Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) funds. Adelino and Dinc (2014) find that financially distressed firms lobbied more for the Stimulus Act and that those firms that lobbied—not necessarily the distressed firms per se—received larger stimulus funds.11Our paper instead studies the actions of banks, not those of politicians.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the institutional background, while SectionIIdescribes the data. SectionIIIpresents results of our empirical analysis, and SectionIVprovides results of robustness analyses. SectionVinvestigates whether delays in foreclosure actions were associated with improvements in other aspects of borrower welfare. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Institutional Background:
We focus on foreclosures initiated during the 111thCongress, from January 2009 through December 2010. Following the literature on the importance of congressional committees in general and the HFSC in particular12for financial institutions, we study the role of membership in the HFSC.
The HFSC was very busy during this period. The issues considered included TARP and similar crisis-related programs, deposit insurance limits, restrictions on executive compensation, credit cards, derivatives clearing houses, and consumer financial protection. The HFSC also played a crucial role in shaping the broadest financial sector reform since the 1933 Glass-Steagall Act, which culminated in the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act.13Moreover, the HFSC was actively involved in investigating various aspects of mortgage servicing markets, including possible irregularities in the foreclosure process, commonly referred to as “robo-signing.”14
Financial institutions were active in this process. The Center for Responsive Politics reports that the financial sector as a whole spent about $459 million on lobbying during the 2009 to 2010 period. Padovani and Gibson (2011) examine the lobbying efforts of banks in regard to the Dodd-Frank bill and find that banks with less traditional businesses spent more money on lobbying, and increased their lobbying efforts after the financial reform proposal was announced.
Servicers of mortgage loans play multiple roles. First, they monitor and receive scheduled periodic payments from a borrower pursuant to the terms of the mortgage contract. Second, they transfer these payments to the owner of the loan. Finally, if the loan is in default, the servicer has the right, subject to contractual limitations with the lender, to engage in a number of loss mitigation activities. These activities may include loan modifications or preforeclosure dispositions (e.g., short sales or deed-in-lieu arrangements) as well as foreclosures.
The foreclosure process works as follows. Typically, upon 90-day delinquency, the lender or servicer issues a “Demand Letter” or “Notice to Accelerate.” The borrower typically has 30 days to pay the full amount due. If the borrower fails to pay the full amount, the lender or servicer may initiate the foreclosure process (through either a judicial, that is, court-supervised, process or a nonjudicial process).15Lenders are not likely to settle with borrowers for less than the full amount due.16
It should be emphasized that the start of foreclosure is not triggered automatically for delinquent loans; rather, it requires action by the loan servicer. Although borrowers may have some power to delay theresolutionof the foreclosure process, especially in judicial foreclosure states, they do not have much power to delay the start other than by making the missed payments.17, 18
Mortgage foreclosure laws are set at the state level, and there exists wide heterogeneity in such laws across states (Pence (2006), Ghent and Kudlyak (2011), Mian, Sufi, and Trebbi (2015)). Some states require a judicial foreclosure process, whereby repossession of the collateral property is overseen by the court. Before the start of foreclosure, the servicer may try to negotiate forbearance options with the borrower. However, if the servicer decides to initiate foreclosure, courts in the judicial foreclosure states start the hearings process to determine the payoff structure for the various lien holders on the mortgage subsequent to the auction of the property. Other states allow lenders wide discretion in foreclosure actions, although lender actions may still be subject to judicial review to ensure their legality. In these states, mortgages include a “power of sale” clause, which allows lenders or servicers to repossess and sell the property in the case of default. Typically, the property is put up for auction and the proceeds go toward paying off the remaining debt.
article.II. Data:
Our main data set consists of loan-level information provided by residential mortgage servicers and collected by McDash Analytics. The data set provides extensive information on loan, property, and borrower characteristics at the time of origination as well as dynamically updated loan information subsequent to origination. The servicer or lender of a loan, however, is not identified. We focus on the loans that became 90 days delinquent for the first time between January 2009 and December 2010, our sample period. Following the literature, we restrict our sample to owner-occupied, single-family, first-lien loans originated in 2005 or after. We include all loans owned by one of the three major types of investors: private securitization trusts, securitization trusts of government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) such as Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, and banks (“portfolio loans”). We drop those loans whose investor type switches after delinquency, as such loans may include loans sold back to the originator due to fraud or early delinquency as discussed by Piskorski, Seru, and Vig (2010). We also omit loans in the top or bottom 1% in terms of appraisal amount, loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, or loan amount, all as of loan origination, to mitigate the effect of outliers.
In addition to loan-level servicer data, we make use of a unique data set that is constructed through a high-quality match between loan records and borrowers’ full credit reports. This data set is known as Equifax's Credit Risk Insight™ Servicing McDash (CRISM). It is structured as a borrower-level panel that matches each loan in the McDash servicing data set with the borrowers' Equifax credit files. The matching algorithm relies on data available only to the credit bureau such as detailed payment histories, which results in a high-quality combined data set. The credit bureau data span the period during which a mortgage loan exists in the servicer data set. This period is further augmented by the six months prior to loan origination and the six months following loan termination (i.e., foreclosure sale or refinancing). For each borrower associated with a given loan, the data set contains time series that capture the borrower's credit score and total debt outstanding in various credit categories, the breakdown of these aggregates into performing and nonperforming components, and the category-specific required monthly payment. In particular, the data set captures home equity lines of credit, auto loans (both from banks and captive auto finance firms), and credit cards. Consequently, we are able to observe performance of existing nonmortgage credit and originations of new credit for all of the delinquent mortgage borrowers in our main sample.
The finest level of geographic data for loans available to us is loans’ zip codes. We match all loans to their congressional districts by zip code using the MABLE/Geocorr2K data set from the Missouri Census Data Center. We drop loans from the zip codes that match multiple congressional districts. We obtain party affiliation and committee assignments of representatives from the data set of Stewart and Woon.19Out of the 435 members of the House of Representatives in the 111thCongress, 71 served on the HFSC—42 Democrats and 29 Republicans. Figure 1 (please refer to internet appendix) provides a map of committee member districts in the contiguous 48 states; Alaska and Hawaii had no representation in the HFSC. The map leads to several observations. First, the committee members represent a broad geographic cross-section of the United States. Second, states with a high proportion of financial institutions such as New York, Massachusetts, and North Carolina are well represented. Finally, some of the states that observe high appreciation of house prices before the crisis such as Nevada and Arizona are not represented in the committee.
A loan's LTV ratio, both at origination and especially during the loan's life, is likely to be an important factor in a servicer's decision to foreclose when the loan becomes delinquent. Unfortunately, only the LTV ratio at origination is provided in our data set. To estimate the LTV ratio during a loan's life, we use the zip code–level Home Price Index provided by CoreLogic. This index is available for at least one zip code for each congressional district except 19 districts, one of which is represented in the HFSC. Consequently, the loans from those districts are omitted from the analysis. TableI, Panel A, reports the number of loans and the number of districts by party affiliation and committee membership.20One key takeaway from this table is that the fraction of loans from HFSC districts (17.2%) is very similar to the fraction of the HFSC member districts (16.9%).
AppendixAsummarizes variable definitions and identifies sources. TableI, Panels B and C, present summary statistics for zip code–level demographic variables and borrower-level variables, respectively, with the latter provided by CRISM. The statistics are reported for the overall sample as well as by the districts of committee and noncommittee members. Differences in subsample averages are also provided. The standard errors for the differences in means are corrected for clustering at the congressional district level.
TableI, Panel B, reports sample statistics for demographic variables such as median income, the fractions of African-American and Hispanic households from the American Community Survey, the unemployment rate from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, and the fraction of the urban population from the 2000 Census. If the data are provided at the county level, we use county-level data. The averages for demographic variables do not differ significantly from each other based on HFSC membership with the exception of median household income and urbanization, both of which are significantly higher in committee member districts, at the 10% and 5% levels, respectively. The table also provides sample statistics on the decrease in the home price index from the loan origination date to the delinquency date using the zip code–level single-home residential price index of CoreLogic. This variable is updated monthly with the contemporaneous index in the regressions. Loans in both noncommittee and committee member districts experienced a similar decline in house prices of 33.5% and 31.6%, respectively, as of the 90-day delinquency date. This difference is not statistically significant.
TableI, Panel C, provides sample statistics at the borrower level obtained from CRISM. We use data on the borrower's FICO score and total nonmortgage borrowing six months before the onset of 90-day mortgage delinquency. We observe no statistically significant difference in sample means between committee and noncommittee districts.
TableI, Panel D, provides sample statistics on loan-level variables. As we only have data on the first-lien LTV ratio as of loan origination, we estimate the contemporaneous LTV ratio by updating the house value using a zip code–level price index. Although Panel D reports this variable as of the month of delinquency, this variable is updated in the regression analyses with contemporaneous index values to capture the continuing changes in house prices during the sample period. Not surprisingly for a sample restricted to delinquent loans, the estimated first-lien LTV ratios are very high, averaging 109%.
Importantly, there is no statistical difference in terms of subsample averages based on committee membership for any of our variables as indicated in the Difference column. Notice that the standard errors reported for those differences are robust to clustering at the congressional district level. Specifically, looking at loan amount, FICO score at origination, estimated LTV ratio, interest rate, and indicators of various mortgage types (jumbo, interest-only, and low grade), there is no difference across noncommittee and committee member districts.
TableI, Panel E, presents the mean time from the onset of 90-day delinquency to the start of foreclosure. These are “restricted” means in the sense that they ignore the fact that, for many loans, the foreclosure process does not commence by the end of the sample period. In other words, these simple calculations ignore the resulting right-censoring and underestimate the mean. Nevertheless, the average time to the start of foreclosures is longer in the committee districts, and the 95% confidence intervals do not overlap.21Perhaps more informatively, we also test for the equality of the distributions of time to foreclosure starts in committee and noncommittee districts. The last column in Panel E provides theχ2test and itsp-value. The test indicates that the distributions of time to the start of foreclosure in committee and noncommittee districts are different and that the difference is highly statistically significant.
article.III. Regression Analysis:
Our identification strategy relies on studying the impact of a political factor that is unlikely to proxy for any economic factor that might affect servicers’ foreclosure decisions. The HFSC membership of the congressperson in whose district the mortgage is located is likely to be such a factor for several reasons. First, unlike party affiliation, which might proxy for many economic and social factors due to gerrymandering of congressional districts, committee memberships are not tied to districts. If an incumbent loses an election, the newly elected politician in that district does not inherit the incumbent's committee seat. Second, each member's likelihood of becoming a committee chair or ranking member depends on the member's senioritywithinthe committee, not on his seniority in the House of Representatives. This rule discourages many members from self-selecting into the HFSC from their previous committees after the crisis started and foreclosures increased in their districts. Third, because of the aforementioned seniority rules, most members in the HFSC became members for the first time well before the onset of the crisis. We perform further checks on this point in the robustness section. Finally, the role of committee membership, especially the role of HFSC membership, for the financial sector has already been well studied though not in this context. Hence, any impact of HFSC membership on foreclosure starts is very likely to be due to political, not economic, reasons.
Focusing on HFSC membership also has disadvantages. First, our identification of politically motivated (in)action by the servicers is relative to their actions in noncommittee districts. This does not rule out political action by the servicers in those noncommittee districts. Rather, we only assume that any political motives of the servicers are likely to be stronger in HFSC districts than in noncommittee districts because the former has more power over the banks as shown in the literature in other contexts. To the extent that some of the servicer behavior is also politically motivated in noncommittee districts, our methodology underestimates politically motivated foreclosure delays in the country. Second, some states and their senators may have greater political power, which might also affect servicer behavior. Following common practice in the political economy literature, we choose to use state fixed effects—or rather, state × time interaction fixed effects—to control for all state-level differences. In that respect, our approach will again underestimate the political effect on foreclosures. Finally, the majority party has more power in the House of Representatives. Unfortunately, congressional districts are not drawn randomly and the party affiliation of the district representative may proxy for unobserved demographic factors. Accordingly, we control for the politician's party affiliation in the analysis but we choose not to interpret its coefficient in political terms. In other words, to the extent that servicer behavior is different in the districts of majority party members, our analysis will capture this difference but not interpret it as political delay.
Loanienters the study in monthti0, which is the first occurrence of the 90-day delinquency status for that loan. The same loan exits the study in monthtiT, which is the earliest occurrence of the start of foreclosure or one of the exit events such as becoming current or no longer being reported. Finally, because a servicer's decision in a given congressional district may not be independent of another decision within the same district, the error terms in the regression above may be correlated within a district, which would lead to underestimated standard errors for the coefficients. Thus, following Bertrand, Duflo, and Mullainathan (2004), all of the errors reported in this study are corrected for clustering at the congressional district level.
Loan-level controls are motivated by the literature. They include indicators for a FICO score above 680 or between 620 and 680 (omitted category: FICO < 620); debt-to-income ratio at origination; indicators for fixed rate and interest-only loans (omitted category: adjustable rate mortgages); indicators for full-documentation and unknown documentation loans (omitted category: no/low documentation); and indicators for jumbo, low grade, and refinance loans, as well as for securitization status (GSE or private label, omitted category: portfolio loans). Following the literature, we also include an indicator variable for the LTV ratio at origination equal to 80% as a proxy for the existence of a second lien on the property. Continuous loan-level variables include the (log of) loan amount, the first interest rate observed, elapsed time from origination to the first classification as 90 days delinquent, and the decrease in the residential home price index for that zip code since loan origination.
Because our main hypothesis variable is based on politics and geography, our regressions also include zip code–level demographic variables in addition to the party affiliation of the congressperson representing that zip code. These variables include (log of) median household income, shares of African- American and Hispanic households, unemployment, urbanization, and the decrease in the home price index since loan origination.
Our main result, namely, that foreclosure starts are delayed in the districts of HFSC members, is presented in TableII. Column (1) reports results for the benchmark specification; it includes all of our control variables, including party affiliation, but not HFSC membership.
The results from the baseline specification are largely intuitive and fall within the broader context of the literature evaluating servicer decisions to modify or foreclose delinquent mortgage loans. The discussion in this literature focuses on servicers’ ability to identify loans that are either likely to self-cure in the absence of any action or likely to redefault even in the event of modification (e.g., Piskorski, Seru, and Vig (2010), Agarwal et al. (2011), Adelino, Gerardi, and Willen (2013), Zhang (2013)). These studies show that foreclosure decisions are influenced by a number of agency frictions that create conflicting incentives between owners of the loans (GSEs or private investors) and entities making modification/foreclosure decisions (servicers). Consistent with these studies, we find that securitized loans are foreclosed more quickly than portfolio loans. The same is true for low-grade loans, loans underwritten without documentation, and nonfixed rate and nonamortizing loans, all of which point to an ex ante higher risk of redefault. These findings are consistent with Agarwal et al. (2011), who use a more detailed data set and find that these borrowers are more likely to experience foreclosure and less likely to be offered loan modifications.
Column (2) of TableIIincludes only party affiliation and our main hypothesis variable,Finance Committee, which is set to 1 if the congressperson representing that zip code is a member of the HFSC. The latter has a negative coefficient that is statistically significant at the 1% level. This suggests that loan servicers delay the start of foreclosure for a delinquent loan if the loan is located in the district of an HFSC member. Note that the analysis also controls nonparametrically for all time-specific state-level factors through state × month fixed effects, so this result is unlikely to be capturing any state-level factors. Also, the errors are robust to clustering at the congressional district level, so the results are unlikely to be due to the large number of observations.
The regression in column (3) adds all of the loan-level and demographic variables. TheFinance Committeevariable again has a negative coefficient that is statistically significant at the 1% level, which confirms the presence of foreclosure delays in committee districts. The magnitude of the delay in committee members’ districts relative to noncommittee members’ districts is about 3.7% ( = 1 – exp(–0.038)). Finally, the regression in column (4) repeats that in column (3) with only state and month fixed effects rather than state × month interaction fixed effects, as some of the estimations presented below do not converge with the latter set of fixed effects. The results are again both quantitatively and qualitatively very similar.
Notice that the magnitude of the coefficient onFinance Committeechanges little from column (2) to columns (3) or (4) even though the latter specifications include many demographic and loan-level variables suggested in the literature. This result suggests that committee membership of the politician is indeed orthogonal to many loan and borrower characteristics as well as zip code demographics. In contrast, the coefficient on the majority party indicator, which may proxy for demographic and economic factors unlike the committee membership indicator, changes substantially and loses significance. In other words, both for the institutional reasons that determine committee membership and for the presence of many financial and demographic control variables, the effect of committee membership that we find is very unlikely to be due to any economic reason. Instead, the delay we find in these districts is likely to be due to political reasons.
For loans that exit afterT, ordinary least squares (OLS) estimation of2cannot distinguish the reason for the exit. For instance, a loan may exit the analysis afterTnot due to the start of foreclosure but because it becomes current (self-cure) or because we do not follow loans beyond the end of our sample period in December 2010 (right-censoring). Thus, unless the foreclosure process commences for all delinquent loans before the end of our sample period, OLS will underestimate the time to the start of foreclosure. We, therefore, need to take the exit reason into account. Accordingly, we estimate regression2using maximum likelihood with the likelihood function that incorporates the reason for exits afterT. The likelihood function depends on the distribution assumed for the error termεi. We estimate2using four distributional assumptions from the literature, namely, normal, logistic, extreme value, and extreme value with a scale parameter.23,24Note that the latter two distributions imply exponential and Weibull hazard functions, which further relate this analysis to that in the previous section (TableII). The regressions include state and month-of-delinquency fixed effects25and standard errors are again clustered at the congressional district level.
TableIIIpresents the results of this analysis. The estimated coefficient onFinance Committeeis positive and statistically significant at the 1% level, which indicates longer time to foreclosure for delinquent loans in the districts of HFSC members. The magnitude of the coefficient estimates ranges from 0.037 to 0.044. Given the logarithmic form of our dependent variable, these estimates suggest an increase of 3.7% to 4.4% in the time to foreclosure in committee districts.
The estimated mean survival time to the start of foreclosure for the full sample ranges from 12.1 to 33.8 months depending on the distribution assumed for the error term. The estimated marginal increase in the mean survival time for the committee districts ranges from 0.5 to 1.5 months.26Because estimates of the mean may be sensitive to the distributional assumptions for the right tail of the error term, we also report the predicted median time, which is a more robust indicator of centrality. The estimated median survival time for the full sample ranges from 7.7 to 8.5 months, while the estimated marginal increase for the median survival time in the committee districts ranges from 0.3 to 0.4 months—again a nonnegligible marginal effect.
We use these results to estimate the dollar amount of the cost of delay to the financial sector and compare it to the campaign contributions to the HFSC members by the largest banks (servicers) that determine the delay. This estimate will naturally be imprecise because we lack information on when banks expected to complete the foreclosure process and what amount they expected to recover from foreclosures. These expectations, which are unobservable to the econometrician, are related to the banks’ expectations about future house price dynamics, which are also unobservable.
Any cost of delay to the lenders is also likely to be underestimated because our test design is geared toward identifying political delays in foreclosure starts, not toward quantifying the total effect of political motivations in foreclosure starts. In particular, our methodology measures the delay in committee districts relative to noncommittee districts and thus is silent about the possibility of political delays in noncommittee districts as well.
Despite these issues related to precision and underestimation, a proverbial “ballpark figure” may still be informative and hence we proceed by following existing work on this topic. Cordell et al. (2015) focus on direct foreclosure costs that vary with the time the loan remains in delinquency and foreclosure. These costs include hazard insurance, property taxes, maintenance and repair, and increased depreciation in house value (Melzer (2017)). Their calculation controls for fixed foreclosure costs and excludes negative externalities to neighboring properties. These delay costs are ultimately borne by the lenders, but the servicers may have to make required payments until liquidation, recovering them only at liquidation. On average, they estimate the total foreclosure delay costs to be about 18% of the unpaid loan balance and the average time from the start of foreclosure to liquidation to be 32.1 months during the period from November 2008 to August 2010. These figures rise to 20% of the loan balance and 33.6 months during the September 2010 to January 2012 period.27We use these costs and lengths of time as the basis for our calculation. For the average delay in committee districts, we use 0.5 months, the lowest estimate in TableIII. Assuming that the total costs of foreclosure delays in Cordell et al. (2015) increases linearly over time, we arrive at a monthly cost estimate of $280 to $300 per $100,000 remaining loan balance on average.28Even with an aggressive assumption of 20% annual cost of capital for the lenders, the present value of monthly delay costs as of the onset of delinquency and evaluated at the average time from delinquency to foreclosure and liquidation is about $170 to $180 per $100,000 of delinquent loan balances.29
Although this may not be a large amount per loan, with $17.1 billion in outstanding delinquent balances in the districts of the HFSC members in our sample, it implies a total nondiscounted cost of about $48 to $51 million at the time of delinquency (or about $30 million using the 20% cost of capital assumption). As one benchmark for comparison, the largest 10 residential mortgage servicers,30which service the large majority of loans in our sample, made campaign contributions of $980,000 to the HFSC members during our sample period.31Theirtotallobbying expenditures for the legislative (both chambers of Congress) and the executive branches of the government was about $44 million in 2009 and 2010.32In other words, the total in-sample cost of delay is an order of magnitude larger than the campaign contributions of the large servicing banks to the HFSC members during our sample period. Indeed, it is more comparable to the lobbying expenses of these firms.
The fact that these costs are substantially greater than the campaign contributions has important political economy implications. During the period of interest, corporate lobbying expenses faced few restrictions, while corporations were subject to limits in their campaign contributions. Notably, lobbying expenses could not legally be channeled to politicians. The delays in foreclosure starts that we document, in contrast, could be targeted directly to the constituents of particular politicians. In fact, in some respects, they could be better targeted to particular politicians’ constituents than some of the mass media political advertisements paid for with campaign contributions, as we discuss in the introduction.
The calculations above are necessarily imprecise. However, they suggest that if the campaign contributions of the largest banks to the HFSC members or their lobbying are politically important, as shown in the political economy literature reviewed in the introduction, the delay in foreclosure starts in committee members’ districts is also politically important.33
article.IV. Robustness:
In this section, we subject our results to several robustness tests. First, we run a series of placebo tests in which we explore foreclosure patterns for members of other committees and for the HFSC members in past periods. Second, we test whether a similar pattern emerges for the onset of delinquency. Third, we examine whether committee members may be self-selecting into the HFSC. Fourth, we check the robustness of our results to alternative econometric procedures. Finally, we test whether the results continue to hold in various subsamples.
We conduct several falsification tests. First, we explore whether differences in the rate of foreclosure initiations are affected by membership on congressional committees that have no ostensible link to housing markets. The three largest committees in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 111thCongress were Transportation & Infrastructure, Financial Services, and Armed Services, in that order. It is unlikely that banks would choose to adjust their servicing practices in the districts of Armed Services or Transportation Committee members. Membership in those committees can thus serve as a good placebo for our analysis because they have very few members in common with the HFSC.34Using the membership on each of these committees in turn, we reestimate our main specification. The results are presented in TableIV, columns (1) and (2). We detect no statistically significant effect of membership in the defense or the transportation committees on servicers’ foreclosure initiation decisions.
Next, in columns (3) and (4) of TableIV, we use foreclosure choices for loans that became delinquent in previous Congresses as placebos. Here, we focus on the 109th(2005 to 2006) and 110th(2007 to 2008) Congresses, as delinquency rates during those years were not as high as in 2009 and 2010 and had not yet become as political an issue as during our main study period. Hence, any bank that was active in mortgage servicing was much less likely to adjust its foreclosure actions to mitigate any legislative concerns it had. Moreover, in previous sessions of Congress, the HFSC did not consider any legislation that was nearly as important as that taken up by the 111thCongress.
We first repeat our main regression specification from TableIIfor 2007 and 2008, focusing on loans that become 90 days delinquent for the first time during this period. We then repeat the analysis for the 2005 to 2006 period. In both cases, we reconstruct our political economy variables to reflect contemporaneous party affiliation and committee membership. The results are reported in TableIV, columns (3) and (4). As expected, the foreclosure decisions on delinquent loans are not measurably different in HFSC members’ districts. The coefficients are statistically insignificant and, for the 2005 to 2006 period, have the opposite sign.
One possible concern is whether legislators self-select into the HFSC based on foreclosure practices in their districts. Reverse causality is not particularly plausible: Legislators are unlikely to choose to be a member of the HFSC just because banks delay foreclosing on their delinquent voters. However, an unobservable factor driving both the committee membership decision of legislators and the foreclosure decisions of banks is more of a concern. For example, if delinquency rates are expected to be high in a district, the legislator representing the district may want to be on the HFSC. An increase in delinquencies in committee districts in itself is not sufficient to drive the pattern we document because our analysis is conditional on the onset of delinquency. However, if the foreclosures are delayed longer due to the large volume of delinquencies in that district, then we could see the effect we observe above.
To rule out this possibility, we extract all of the loans that are current and have never experienced delinquency as of December 2008 and follow them through December 2010. We then study the rate of 30- and 90-day delinquency in a hazard model.35We use the same control variables as in our main analysis. The results are reported in TableV. We find no statistical difference in the rate of delinquencies in districts of HFSC members. In other words, our results are unlikely to be driven mechanically by differential rates of delinquencies in committee members’ districts or by the self-selection of legislators based on expected delinquencies.
The institutional features of congressional committees provide us another test for the possibility of self-selection by legislators into the HFSC. Senior committee members tend to be more powerful, and legislators receive seniority based on their service tenure in a given committee. When legislators leave or switch committees, they lose their seniority in the committee even if they remain in Congress. In fact, all 32 members of the HFSC in the 108thCongress (2003 to 2004) that were still in the U.S. House in the 111thCongress remained on the committee. In other words, many members of the 2009 to 2010 HFSC made the decision to be in the committee well before the foreclosure crisis. We use this feature to address concerns of self-selection into the HFSC. Whatever the economic determinants of banks’ foreclosure decisions might have been in 2009 and 2010, they are likely to be orthogonal to the committee membership decision made years before.
The regression in column (1) of TableVIrepeats our main specification after excluding all of the HFSC members who joined the committee in 2005 or later. The second column repeats our main specification after excluding all of the representatives who first took office in the House in 2005 or after. Our main results are robust to these exclusions: the foreclosure of delinquent loans is delayed in the districts of HFSC members. Indeed, the magnitude of this political effect becomes even larger, which suggests that the political effect we detect may be stronger in the districts of senior members.36
Our sample runs through the end of 2010, but in the latter part of that year the practice of  “robo-signing” came to widespread attention. This practice refers to cases in which the large servicing banks seemed to process large numbers of foreclosure documents in a short time without legally required individual attention. Although it would be interesting in and of itself if the servicing banks reacted differently in committee districts to increased public scrutiny of this issue, it is also desirable to check the robustness of our results to the exclusion of that period. We therefore repeat our main regression by stopping the analysis period first at the end of December 2009 and then at the end of March 2010, in both cases well before robo-signing attracted attention. We present the results in TableVII, columns (1) and (2). Our main results continue to hold both quantitatively and qualitatively after excluding the latter part of our sample period. It is also worth noting that all of our specifications include state-month interaction fixed effects, which would pick up state-level delays caused by robo-signing.
We also check the robustness of our results to different econometric methods. First, we estimate our main regression in TableII, column (3) using the Cox proportional hazard model. The Cox hazard model has the advantage of allowing arbitrary duration dependence (baseline hazard). We report the results in TableVII, column (3). The coefficient on theFinance committeeindicator is very similar to that reported in TableIIin terms of both magnitude and statistical significance. Next, we repeat that regression using the Weibull distribution and report the results in column (4). We again obtain very similar results both quantitatively and qualitatively.
A possible concern with our results is that we may be comparing foreclosure rates in noncommittee member districts that are rural to those in committee member districts that are urban within a given state despite including the urbanization rate of the congressional district in the control variables. Although we control for the proportion of urban population in the regressions, because the housing crisis affected urban areas disproportionately, it is still important to check the robustness of our results by limiting the comparison to zip codes that are near one another. Hence, in TableVII, columns (6)–(8), we estimate our main regression from TableII, column (3), but now we require all of the noncommittee zip codes to be within 10, 25, and 50 miles of a committee zip code in the same state and vice versa. This significantly reduces the sample size. For example, our 10-mile sample in column (6) is less than a quarter of the original sample that we use in our main regression in TableII, column (3). However, in all three specifications, our results remain both economically and statistically significant.
We also examine subsamples by loan characteristics. In Table IA.I of the Internet Appendix,37we purge different sample subsets. The results hold in all of the subsamples considered. In column (1), we examine only those mortgages that are classified as “grade A” (or nonlow grade) by the servicers. In column (2), we restrict the sample to loans with maturity of 15, 20, and 30 years at origination. These are the most common maturities, and loans with other maturities may be nonstandard on other dimensions as well. In column (3), we drop from the analysis October 2010 and thereafter, as that period includes both the “lame duck” months of November and December 2010 as well as October 2010, when the news about robo-signing broke. Our results continue to hold in all of the subsamples: Foreclosures of delinquent loans are delayed in the districts of HFSC members, with the magnitudes comparable across these specifications.
Our identification strategy is already based on a factor that has a highly political nature but little, if any, economic nature. The robustness checks above allow us to further rule out economic explanations for the delay in foreclosures. Hence, we interpret the delay in foreclosure initiations as due to loan servicers’ political concerns.
article.V. Welfare and Potential Economic Channe:
It is conceivable that loan servicers granted foreclosure delays in HFSC member's districts in order to allow delinquent borrowers residing in such districts to regain their financial footing and remedy their delinquencies. If this were the case, we should find superior performance on existing nonmortgage credit obligations among delinquent mortgage borrowers in such districts. Similarly, we should find that these borrowers were also more successful in obtaining new nonmortgage credit. The CRISM data set that uniquely links mortgage loans with the rest of a borrower's credit records allows us to test this hypothesis. In particular, we analyze performance and origination of credit in several categories: auto loans, credit cards, as well as student loans and retail (store card) credit for those borrowers whose mortgages are 90 days delinquent during 2009 to 2010 and thus form the sample in our main analysis above.
We begin our analysis by documenting the relative importance of various categories of nonmortgage borrowing in Table IA.VII, Panel A, of the Internet Appendix. Credit card balances account for nearly half of outstanding nonmortgage debt among the delinquent borrowers in our sample, while auto loans make up about 29% of nonmortgage balances. More importantly, there are no statistically significant differences between shares of various nonmortgage obligations across member and nonmember districts.
Panel B reports delinquency rates on nonmortgage credit for borrowers in our sample, all of whom are delinquent on their mortgages at the time they enter the sample. The panel thus documents incidence rates of nonmortgage delinquency during the two-year period following mortgage delinquency. Starting with auto loans, we find an incidence rate of 2.54% for auto loan delinquency, where the incidence rate is the number of loans that fall into delinquency divided by the total number of months loans remain in our sample until delinquency. The incidence rate is very similar across committee member and nonmember districts. The incidence rate of credit card delinquencies is somewhat higher at 3.85% for the entire sample, but the incidence rates are virtually identical for the member and nonmember subgroups. We find similar results for all other nonmortgage loans, which cover student debt, retail, and consumer finance, as well as for the aggregate encompassing all nonmortgage loan obligations.
Panel C repeats the analysis of incidence rates but focuses on the origination of new credit, as opposed to the performance of existing obligations. The data suggest that mortgage delinquency does not necessarily exclude a borrower from participation in credit markets. The incidence rates for new auto loans and credit card accounts represent 11.4% and 15.0% of borrower-month observations, respectively. The incidence rates of new instances of other nonmortgage debt are higher still at 21.6%. This finding is not surprising, since this category includes student debt, most of which is not underwritten as it is provided independent of borrower credit standing. The differences in rates between member and nonmember districts are mixed: member districts display a higher incidence of new auto loans, a lower incidence of new bank credit cards, and an almost identical incidence of other nonmortgage debt. However, none of these differences meets conventional levels of statistical significance.
The summary statistics suggest that delinquent borrowers in HFSC member districts, which are found to experience foreclosure delays in the earlier analysis, did not experience favorable credit outcomes in nonmortgage categories. We next turn to a more rigorous regression-based hazard analysis to evaluate the validity of these results.
Table IA.VIII presents results for nonmortgage performance using the hazard regression framework developed in Section III.A. In each of the specifications, we estimate the hazard rate of being 90 days delinquent on a given type of existing nonmortgage obligation. Each specification employs a set of loan, geography, and time controls that are identical to those described in Section III.A. For all loan categories except auto loans our main variable of interest,Finance committee, is a statistically insignificant factor in the hazard analysis. Moreover, the point estimates of this variable change signs from one category to the next. For auto loans, the coefficient is positive and statistically significant at the 10% level, which indicates that these loans are more likely to become delinquent in committee districts; this is the opposite of what might be expected if banks were delaying foreclosures to increase borrower welfare. Thus, we find no evidence that the foreclosure delays in committee districts allowed delinquent mortgage borrowers in those districts to stay current in nonmortgage loans.
It is also possible that any potential benefits from foreclosure delays may have been concentrated among the most vulnerable borrowers. To test this conjecture, in Table IA.IX, Panel A, of the Internet Appendix, we repeat our analysis for borrowers with a low (below-median) FICO score at the time of sample entry, while in Panel B we limit attention to borrowers who failed to fully document their income when they obtained their mortgage.38Both of these groups can be thought of as among the most vulnerable borrowers, and hence may have a different response to foreclosure delays in committee member districts.
Using both of these subsamples, we fail to find a beneficial (negative) effect of being in an HFSC member's district. The only statically significant results we obtain for low-documentation borrowers—for auto loan delinquency and for all other loans—point in the opposite direction. In other words, we find no evidence that foreclosure delays in committee districts allowed delinquent mortgage borrowers to stay current in nonmortgage loans either in the full sample or in subsamples of credit-constrained borrowers.
Turning attention to the origination of new credit, we next present results of hazard analysis in Table IA.X of the Internet Appendix. Once again, we fail to detect a statistically significant beneficial (this time positive) effect of being in an HFSC member's district. We repeat this analysis for borrower subsamples in the same fashion as for nonmortgage delinquencies. The results in Table IA.XI of the Internet Appendix also indicate the absence of any measurable effect of being in an HFSC member's district.
To summarize, the results of the analysis in this section fail to provide support for the hypothesis that foreclosure delays in HFSC member districts led to welfare gains for the affected borrowers. In particular, there is no evidence for improved performance on nonmortgage credit or for obtaining new nonmortgage credit. Instead, the results confirm that servicers were motivated by political, not economic, reasons to delay the start of foreclosures in the districts of HFSC members.
Having documented a delay in foreclosure initiations, we attempt to disentangle potential economic mechanisms by exploiting several sources of cross-sectional variation.39In particular, we consider the role of banking market competitiveness, consumer protection laws, the degree of political competitiveness, and the costs of foreclosure delays. Heterogeneity in these measures across states may be reflected in potential costs and benefits of foreclosure delays. We report results of this analysis in Table IA.XII of the Internet Appendix.
We start with the hypothesis that more competitive banking markets may motivate servicers to delay foreclosures as they fight to enhance their relative competitive position. To test this conjecture, we capture the degree of banking market competitiveness by computing Herfindahl-Hirschman index (HHI) values at the metropolitan statistical area (MSA) level using FDIC Summary of Deposits data for 2008. We then split the sample along the median HHI value and estimate our baseline specification (equation1as implemented in regression (3) of TableII) for each of the resulting subsamples. We find that foreclosure delays are somewhat higher and more precisely estimated in less competitive markets, inconsistent with the null hypothesis.
To further exploit cross-sectional variation in the costs and benefits of delaying, we partition the sample between states with judicial foreclosure review processes and states with nonjudicial foreclosures. The former group has experienced much lengthier foreclosure timelines, and so a politically motivated delay is relatively less costly. However, we find statistically significant evidence of politically motivated delays in both types of states (Table IA.XII of the Internet Appendix, columns (3) and (4)). The strength of this effect is somewhat larger in the nonjudicial states, although the difference in point estimates is not statistically significant.
We next test the hypothesis that lenders might benefit more from targeted delays in states where existing consumer-level protections are stronger. We identify the presence of antipredatory lending laws (APL) at the state level using the classification in Ding et al. (2012). The subsample analysis reported in columns (5) and (6) of Table IA.XII in the Internet Appendix suggests somewhat weaker effects in states with active APL statutes, which is not quite consistent with the notion that servicer actions are more pervasive in states where they are already under higher scrutiny. Part of the explanation for the apparent lack of cross-sectional variation in foreclosure delays with APL laws may be that all of the servicers in our data set have a large national footprint and that the legislation under consideration was national in scope.
As a final cross-sectional check, we contrast congressional districts with varying levels of political competitiveness, as measured by the margin of victory. Using the results of the November 2008 election, only 2% of loans in our sample come from districts in which the margin of victory was less than two percentage points. Raising the win threshold to 5% increases our measure of loans in “competitive districts” to only 7% of the original sample. The last two columns of Table IA.XII in the Internet Appendix report results using the 10% victory margin. In this case, 14% of the loans are in less than 10% districts. However, there is not enough statistical power to identify the political foreclosure delay in a relatively small sample of quasi-competitive districts. We conclude that the extreme skewness of electoral victory margins makes it very difficult to evaluate the hypothesis of greater political benefits for foreclosure delays in more competitive districts.
In sum, the data limitations of our paper make it difficult to take a stand on the mechanisms through which banks link their foreclosure actions and their political concerns. For example, politicians may pressure banks for delays as reported in the introduction. Alternatively, banks may agree on the delay voluntarily, as again reported in the introduction. Our test design and the available data do not allow us to distinguish among the various mechanisms. More importantly, multiple mechanisms may, in fact, result in actions taken simultaneously by different agents in equilibrium. For example, Kroszner and Stratmann (1998) provide a theory of congressional committees and interest groups in which interest groups provide campaign contributions for favorable legislation. Crucially, they also show that this equilibrium exists only if the interest groups and politicians interact repeatedly, which is facilitated by the committee structure in Congress. The authors also provide empirical evidence from the HFSC (then called the Banking Committee). In our case, foreclosure delays have a role akin to campaign contributions. As a repeated game, equilibrium is likely to break down if the chance of reelection is low for the politicians, in which case we can expect to seebothof the mechanisms mentioned above. In other words, potential mechanisms may not be competing explanations of how the delay we document originates, but rather may be complementary and simultaneous aspects of a single equilibrium phenomenon.40
article.VI. Conclusion:
This paper is the first to document an effect of political motivations on foreclosure decisions by banks in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis. Our results show that mortgage-servicing banks delayed the start of foreclosures for delinquent mortgages in the districts of U.S. HFSC members. There was no difference in the onset of delinquencies based on committee membership, however. This result is robust to controlling for many loan- and zip code–level factors and time-specific state-level fixed effects. We do not find a similar effect in our placebo tests using membership in the Transportation & Infrastructure and Armed Services Committees or in previous periods when foreclosures were not a major political issue. Our results are also unlikely to be driven by concerns about sharing the stimulus funds because the HFSC did not have jurisdiction over how those funds were allocated.
Our calculations suggest that the cost of delay to financial institutions is an order of magnitude larger than the campaign contributions to the HFSC by the 10 largest loan servicers during our sample period. Our calculations further imply that the cost of delay is on par with their lobbying expenses. These estimates are based on in-sample figures and thus are fairly conservative.
In addition to contributing to the finance literature on mortgages and foreclosures, our work has implications for the political economy literature. Most of the political economy literature on political influences focuses on campaign contributions and lobbying, which depend on explicit direct expenditures and which are subject to disclosure requirements. The political delays we document point instead to an “in-kind” political activity by firms through their day-to-day operations. The foreclosure delays we document may be more effective than campaign contributions in some respects because the delays can precisely target a politician's constituents. In contrast, some campaign activities like election-time television advertisements, one of the biggest expenditures that campaign contributions finance, cover a geographic area that also includes neighboring districts and thus are less precisely targeted. Lobbying expenditures, however, are not directly captured by the politicians and cannot be legally directed to election campaigns.
One question that arises is why politicians prefer a delay in foreclosures as indicated by the quotes in the introduction. After all, they may also have nondelinquent constituents in their district. Although nondelinquent homeowners may have different incentives in general, they may prefer that large numbers of foreclosures not take place in their neighborhood due to the local negative externalities of foreclosures (Campbell, Giglio, and Pathak (2011)). However, even if nondelinquent borrowers prefer rapid foreclosures, the benefit they obtain from an expeditious process is likely to be diffuse across the nondelinquent owners. In contrast, the benefit of delay in foreclosures will be concentrated among delinquent borrowers and thus the classic interest group arguments of Olson (1965) suggest that politicians are likely to favor the delinquent borrowers.
We focus on the equilibrium outcome instead of equilibrium strategies due to the lack of detailed data. However, the basic premise of the paper only requires that elected officials be aware of foreclosure practices in their districts and that lenders or loan servicers be aware of such interest. Both of these assumptions are quite plausible during the financial crisis period. Politicians are likely to learn about foreclosures in their districts directly from their constituents, or from a number of nonprofit organizations that focus on housing-related issues.41For their part, large financial institutions are likely to be aware of publicity surrounding foreclosure activities.
Our paper does not focus or provide any evidence on what, if anything, the banks received in return for delaying foreclosures. One potential future research area might be to study the returns to banks from their political activities.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
article.Appendix B: Key Dates in the Passage of:
June 17, 2009: Obama Administration releases its financial reform proposal.
July to December 2009: Discussions and hearings in the House Financial Services Committee on the proposal.
December 11, 2009: The U.S. House of Representatives passes the final version.
January to May 2010: The Senate considers the financial reform proposal.
May 20, 2010: The Senate passes its own version and the bill moves to the conference committee.
June 29, 2010: The unified bill leaves the conference committee.
June 30, 2010: The House passes the bill.
July 15, 2010: The Senate passes the bill.
July 21, 2010: The President signs the bill and it becomes law.
